label: delphi-r2-p4
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

In the City of Rio Robles (invented), Councilmember Dana Okafor's spouse is the salaried Director of Programs (~$95,000/yr) at Harbor Light Alliance, a 501(c)(3) homeless-services provider. Under a 2019 city resolution, Okafor also sits on Harbor Light's board of directors, uncompensated, as the council's designated liaison.

The council is about to (a) adopt the framework and scoring criteria for a competitive RFP allocating $2.3M in combined city general-fund and state HHAP pass-through money for homelessness services, and then (b) award the resulting contract(s). Harbor Light is one of three realistic bidders and has historically won about 60% of the city's homelessness contracts. The spouse's position is funded out of Harbor Light's general operating budget, to which city contracts contribute roughly 40%.

Okafor asks the city attorney: May I vote on the RFP framework? On the award? Must I resign the board seat or must my spouse's employment change? And if I simply recuse, is the city safe contracting with Harbor Light?

## The question

Analyze under Government Code § 1090 (including whether board membership plus a spouse's salaried employment is a prohibited financial interest 'in' the contract, whether any § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest exception applies, and whether recusal even cures § 1090 for a board member), and under the Political Reform Act (§§ 87100, 87103 — community-property interest in spouse's income, materiality, and the public-generally exception), plus common-law conflict doctrine. What combination of resignation, recusal, wall procedures, or contract structuring lets the city lawfully fund Harbor Light, if any? What are the consequences of getting it wrong (§ 1090 voidness, disgorgement, criminal exposure, FPPC enforcement)?

## Proposition to assess

"If the city awards the homelessness-services contract to Harbor Light Alliance while Okafor holds the board seat and her spouse holds her position — even with Okafor fully recused from the RFP and the award — the contract would be void or voidable under Government Code § 1090."

This is round 2 of a Delphi panel. Your previous answer:

```json
{
  "probability": 68,
  "confidence": "medium",
  "position_summary": "More likely than not the proposition is correct: absent Okafor's resignation from Harbor Light's board (and possibly a change in her spouse's compensation structure), recusal from the RFP and award votes alone probably does not cure a \u00a7 1090 problem, because (1) Okafor's own board seat is a personal governance-level interest that sits uneasily with the narrow \u00a7 1091.5(a) nonprofit-officer 'noninterest' exception, and (2) the spouse's salary, while nominally fixed, is tied closely enough to city-contract revenue (40% of Harbor Light's budget, 60% historical win rate) that it plausibly falls outside the 'compensation not dependent on the contract' limitation built into \u00a7 1091(b)'s remote-interest categories. Under Thomson v. Call and its progeny, a body member's disqualifying interest voids the contract regardless of recusal from the vote unless a statutory remote-interest (with disclosure+abstention) or noninterest exception cleanly applies \u2014 and on these compounding, repeat-awardee facts I think it more likely than not no clean exception saves the deal without resignation. That said, a genuine, non-frivolous counterargument exists that with (a) resignation from the board, (b) rigorous procedural recusal satisfying \u00a7 1091(a), and (c) confirmation the spouse's pay is a true fixed non-contingent employee salary, the city could lawfully proceed \u2014 but the proposition as framed keeps the board seat and the spousal job intact, which is the weaker fact pattern for lawfulness.",
  "rationales": [
    "Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 holds that a governing-body member's disqualifying financial interest voids the contract regardless of that member's recusal from the vote, because \u00a7 1090's prohibition runs to the whole body, not just to the interested member's ballot.",
    "Okafor's uncompensated Harbor Light board seat is arguably a personal, governance-level financial interest that is not obviously covered by the narrow nonprofit-officer 'noninterest' exception in \u00a7 1091.5(a), particularly because as a council-designated liaison/director she retains influence over the very entity receiving city funds.",
    "The spouse's ostensibly 'fixed' $95k salary is plausibly not truly independent of this contract given that 40% of Harbor Light's operating budget derives from city contracts and Harbor Light has won ~60% of such awards historically, which cuts against the 'compensation not dependent on the contract' premise underlying \u00a7 1091(b) remote-interest treatment of spousal employees.",
    "California courts construe \u00a7 1090 strictly and resolve close calls against public officials because the statute targets the temptation and appearance of self-dealing, not just actual corruption, making a clean judicial finding of a full statutory cure less likely on these aggravated, compounding facts.",
    "A real counter-scenario exists \u2014 if the spouse is a genuine rank-and-file, non-officer employee whose pay is demonstrably unaffected by this specific award, and Okafor's board seat independently qualifies as a \u00a7 1091.5(a) noninterest, then disclosure plus rigorous multi-stage recusal (not just an abstained vote) could validly cure the conflict without resignation, meaning the proposition could be false \u2014 this keeps my estimate below high-confidence territory."
  ],
  "biggest_uncertainty": "Whether Okafor's uncompensated Harbor Light board seat actually qualifies for a \u00a7 1091.5(a) 'noninterest' exception and whether the spouse's salary counts as a true \u00a7 1091(b) 'remote interest' (compensation not dependent on the contract) despite Harbor Light's 40% revenue dependency on city contracts and its 60% historical win rate \u2014 I could not confidently verify the exact statutory subsections/conditions, and the outcome turns entirely on whether these fact-sensitive exceptions apply cleanly to this compounded board-seat-plus-spousal-salary situation.",
  "authorities": [
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 1090(a) (general prohibition; contract void if violated)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 1091 (remote interests; disclosure and abstention requirements) \u2014 exact subsection defining spousal/employee remote interest needs verification",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 1091.5(a) (noninterests, including possible nonprofit-officer category) \u2014 exact subsection and conditions need verification",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 1092 and \u00a7 1097 (civil voidance remedy and criminal penalties for willful violation)",
    "Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 (recusal does not cure a body member's direct disqualifying interest)",
    "Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565 (broad construction of 'financially interested')",
    "People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289 (scope/willfulness under \u00a7 1090)",
    "Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 Cal.4th 1050 (multiple-interest and remote/noninterest exception analysis)",
    "Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (2010) 186 Cal.App.4th 1114 (void vs. voidable discussion)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7\u00a7 87100, 87103 and Fam. Code \u00a7 760 (PRA disqualification and community-property interest in spouse's income) \u2014 relevant to a parallel/overlapping PRA analysis not the \u00a7 1090 holding itself",
    "AG opinions addressing council-designated liaisons/board members of contracting nonprofits (specific pinpoint cites unverified)"
  ]
}
```

The anonymized panel summary (statistics + themes, no attribution):

# Delphi Panel — Round 1 Feedback Summary

## 1. Statistics

Panel of 7. Probability that the proposition is correct: median 72.0, mean 73.0, std dev 4.6, range 67.0–78.0, middle 50% of panel between 68.0 and 78.0.

*(For the skew notes below, "lower-probability" = the three panelists at 67–70; "higher-probability" = the four panelists at 72–78.)*

## 2. Rationale Themes

**A. Recusal cannot cure a body member's § 1090 interest (Thomson v. Call / Stigall structural rule) — 7 of 7.** Every panelist invoked the rule that § 1090's prohibition runs to the contracting body as a whole, so an interested member's abstention/recusal from the vote does not itself validate the contract. No skew.

**B. Spouse's salary as an imputable disqualifying financial interest — 7 of 7.** All panelists treated the spouse's ~$95K salary as the central candidate interest. Within this theme, **5 panelists** (2 lower, 3 higher) explicitly grounded imputation in community-property law (Fam. Code §§ 751/760).

**C. Revenue-dependence nexus (40% city-contract funding / 60% historical win rate) — 6 of 7.** Six panelists (2 lower, 4 higher) cited these quantitative facts as pushing the spousal interest toward "direct/material" rather than remote or speculative. One lower-probability panelist did not rely on the percentages.

**D. Board seat likely separately excepted, but the exception does not reach the spousal salary — 6 of 7.** Six panelists reasoned that Okafor's own uncompensated board seat may fall within a statutory exception (most citing a § 1091.5 noninterest; one citing § 1091 remote-interest treatment), while emphasizing that any such exception would not cover the distinct spousal-employment interest. One lower-probability panelist took the opposite view on the board seat (see Disagreements, point 1).

**E. Acknowledged counterargument: fixed, non-contingent salary may be remote or too attenuated — 7 of 7.** Every panelist identified some version of this as the principal reason their probability was not higher. Panelists differ on where this doubt sits analytically (see Disagreements, points 2–3).

**F. No enumerated § 1091/1091.5 category squarely covers spousal employment with the contracting entity — 4 of 7** (1 lower, 3 higher; slight skew toward higher-probability panelists). These panelists asserted affirmatively that the exception catalog does not reach the spousal salary, rather than treating exception-fit as an open question.

**G. Strict/broad construction of "financially interested," resolving doubts toward disqualification — 4 of 7** (2 lower, 2 higher). Citing Stigall, Honig, and the statute's prophylactic purpose.

**H. Contrast with the Political Reform Act (recusal cures § 87100 but not § 1090) — 3 of 7 as a rationale** (all at or above the median; two additional panelists cited PRA provisions in authorities "for contrast" only). Skews toward higher-probability panelists.

**I. Void/structural remedy under § 1092 (with disgorgement and § 1097 criminal exposure) as reinforcing that recusal is insufficient by design — 3 of 7**, all at 78. Skews toward higher-probability panelists.

**J. Seniority of the spouse's role (Director of Programs, management/policy-adjacent) as analytically significant — 2 of 7**, both at 78. Skews toward higher-probability panelists.

**K. Practical enforcement likelihood (losing-bidder or taxpayer standing under CCP § 526a) — 1 of 7.**

**L. Okafor's fiduciary duty to Harbor Light / dual-loyalty structural harm while shaping RFP criteria — 1 of 7.**

**M. Cure scenarios involving board resignation — 2 of 7**, reaching different conclusions (see Disagreements, point 4).

## 3. Points of Genuine Disagreement

1. **Statutory classification of Okafor's own board seat.** Three incompatible readings appear: (a) most panelists treat the uncompensated board seat as a likely § 1091.5 "noninterest"; (b) one panelist classifies it as a § 1091 remote interest curable by disclosure-and-abstention; (c) one panelist argues it sits uneasily with the narrow § 1091.5(a) exception because it is a personal, governance-level interest carrying influence over the funded entity.

2. **Whether any § 1091(b) remote-interest category could reach the spousal salary at all.** Some panelists treat a spousal-employment/fixed-salary remote-interest route as a genuinely available path that could flip the outcome if properly invoked; others flatly state that no enumerated remote-interest or noninterest category covers a spouse's paid employment with the contracting nonprofit. These are incompatible readings of the exception catalog, not just different emphasis.

3. **Locus of the threshold doubt.** Panelists split on where the pivotal uncertainty lies: (a) whether the fixed, general-operating-budget salary is a § 1090 "financial interest" at all (with one panelist framing the alternative as a pure PRA §§ 87100/87103 materiality question, where recusal would suffice), versus (b) assuming the interest exists and locating the uncertainty entirely in exception fit. These framings assign the same doubt to different analytical steps with different doctrinal consequences.

4. **What cure package (if any) short of the spouse leaving employment could work.** One panelist posits that board resignation + rigorous multi-stage recusal + confirmation of a genuine rank-and-file, non-contingent salary could make the contract lawful; another states that board resignation plus recusal likely would not cure the problem while the spouse remains employed on city-funded dollars.

5. **Whether the spouse's job title/seniority matters to exception fit.** Two panelists condition exception availability on the spouse being rank-and-file rather than a director-level employee; others analyze only the fixed-vs-contingent structure of compensation without treating role seniority as relevant.

## 4. Authorities

**Cited by all 7 panelists:**
- Gov. Code § 1090
- Gov. Code § 1091 (remote interests)
- Gov. Code § 1091.5 (noninterests) — several panelists flagged the exact subsection/text as unverified
- Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633
- Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565
- People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289

**Cited by 5:**
- Gov. Code § 1092 (voidness remedy)
- Gov. Code §§ 87100/87103 (Political Reform Act, mostly cited "for contrast")

**Cited by 4:**
- Fam. Code § 760 (community property)
- Fraser-Yamor v. County of Del Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201 — three cited it as "Fraser-Yamor Agency"; one as "Fraser-Yamor Chevrolet" with a flag to verify the citation

**Cited by 3:**
- Gov. Code § 1097 (criminal penalties)
- Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 Cal.4th 1050

**Cited by 2:**
- Unspecified California Attorney General opinions (one panelist referencing opinions on council-designated liaisons/board members; another referencing opinions on spousal employment with a contracting party; both flagged pinpoint cites as unverified)

**Cited by only 1:**
- Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (2010) 186 Cal.App.4th 1114
- People v. Superior Court (Sahlolbei) (2004) 32 Cal.4th 1223
- Code Civ. Proc. § 526a (taxpayer suits)
- Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae (1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222
- Fam. Code § 751

---

*Panelists: please review the themes, disagreements, and authorities above and submit your round-2 probability and any revised rationale. This summary organizes round-1 responses only; it does not endorse or weight any position.*

Independently reconsider. Do NOT defer to the majority; update only where the summary contains reasons you judge valid, and hold your position where it does not. Explain in response_to_panel which consideration moved you or why none did.

response_to_panel.description: which panel consideration moved you, or why none did

text: {"probability":74,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"Revising upward from 68 to 74: the proposition is more likely than not correct. Two structural pillars remain decisive: (1) under Thomson v. Call, a governing-body member's own disqualifying § 1090 interest voids the contract regardless of that member's recusal from the vote, because the prohibition runs to the body's act of contracting, not just to the interested member's ballot; and (2) on these compounding facts (spouse holds a Director-level, non-rank-and-file position; Harbor Light derives ~40% of its operating budget from city contracts; Harbor Light has won ~60% of prior awards), the 'fixed salary, independent of the contract' characterization needed to fit any § 1091(b) remote-interest cure is genuinely strained. What moved me from round 1 is a point I underweighted: Okafor's interest in her spouse's salary is plausibly not merely a Political Reform Act materiality question but a direct § 1090 'financially interested' predicate in its own right, grounded in community-property law (Fam. Code § 760) and, if accurately recalled, precedent applying that theory to void a public contract benefiting a spouse's employer (Fraser-Yamor Agency v. County of Del Norte, cite unverified). That reframes the analysis: even if Okafor's own board seat is separately cured via a § 1091.5(a) nonprofit-officer noninterest, that cure does not reach the distinct, likely-uncured spousal-salary interest — and the proposition's facts keep both the board seat and the spousal job intact, changing nothing but recusal. The proposition's disjunctive 'void or voidable' framing is also a comparatively easy bar to clear. I stay below the top of the panel's range because the exact statutory text of § 1091(b)'s remote-interest categories and § 1091.5(a)'s conditions remains unverified by me, and a non-frivolous 'fixed, non-contingent salary is too attenuated to be an interest at all' counterargument survives every panelist's own scrutiny, including mine.","rationales":["Thomson v. County of Del Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201 in fact applied this theory to void a contract benefiting a spouse's employer (citation and holding unverified), that is squarely on point.","Separating the two interests analytically (as several panelists did) clarifies the outcome: even assuming Okafor's own uncompensated board seat independently qualifies for a § 1091.5(a) nonprofit-officer/director noninterest, that exception is personal to her governance role and does not extend to cure the distinct financial interest created by her spouse's compensated employment with the same contracting nonprofit — and the proposition's facts do not include any change to that employment, only recusal.","The spouse's role as Director of Programs (a management/policy-adjacent position) makes the 'fixed salary wholly independent of the contract' characterization needed for a § 1091(b) remote-interest cure harder to sustain than it would be for a rank-and-file employee, especially layered onto the 40%-of-budget/60%-win-rate revenue-dependence facts, which push the interest toward 'direct and material' rather than remote or speculative under the strict-construction rule of Stigall and People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289.","The proposition is phrased disjunctively ('void or voidable'), which is an easier bar to satisfy than a pure voidness claim; combined with § 1092's remedial structure and § 1097's criminal exposure for willful violations (both of which exist precisely because the Legislature intended prophylactic, not merely compensatory, consequences), this modestly raises the probability that some legally cognizable defect attaches to the contract as structured, even if courts might differ on the void-versus-voidable characterization."],"biggest_uncertainty":"I cannot independently verify (a) the exact text and conditions of Gov. County of Del Norte, which the panel repeatedly invoked (with some internal disagreement over the case name) as direct authority applying community-property theory to void a § 1090 contract via a spouse's employer — if that case does not actually stand for that proposition, a meaningful part of my upward revision loses its footing.","authorities":["Gov. County of Del Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201 (possible direct authority on spousal community-property interest voiding a § 1090 contract) — citation, name, and holding all unverified","Fam. Code §§ 760 and 751 (community property in spousal earnings) — relevance to § 1090 'financially interested' standard, not just PRA, needs doctrinal confirmation","Gov. Code § 760) supplies a direct § 1090 'financially interested' theory — not merely a Political Reform Act materiality argument — was a real gap in my round-1 reasoning, where I had parked Fam. Code § 760 as 'relevant to the PRA analysis, not the § 1090 holding.' If courts treat spousal income as the official's own interest for § 1090 purposes (as Fraser-Yamor arguably held, though unverified), that meaningfully strengthens the case that an interest exists at all, which was my own stated principal source of doubt. Second, the panel's cleaner analytical separation — board seat possibly cured under § 1091.5(a), but that cure not reaching the distinct spousal-salary interest — is a sharper and more defensible structure than my round-1 treatment, which conflated uncertainty about the board seat with uncertainty about the spousal interest. I did not move all the way to the panel mean/median because the core statutory-text uncertainty (exact § 1091(b)/§ 1091.5(a) conditions) that anchored my round-1 score is unresolved by the summary itself — several panelists flagged the same gap — and the 7/7 acknowledged counterargument (fixed salary as too attenuated to be a disqualifying interest) still represents a live, non-frivolous path to the proposition being false that I'm not willing to discount below roughly one-in-four.","changed":true}

data.position_summary: Revising upward from 68 to 74: the proposition is more likely than not correct. Two structural pillars remain decisive: (1) under Thomson v. Call, a governing-body member's own disqualifying § 1090 interest voids the contract regardless of that member's recusal from the vote, because the prohibition runs to the body's act of contracting, not just to the interested member's ballot; and (2) on these compounding facts (spouse holds a Director-level, non-rank-and-file position; Harbor Light derives ~40% of its operating budget from city contracts; Harbor Light has won ~60% of prior awards), the 'fixed salary, independent of the contract' characterization needed to fit any § 1091(b) remote-interest cure is genuinely strained. What moved me from round 1 is a point I underweighted: Okafor's interest in her spouse's salary is plausibly not merely a Political Reform Act materiality question but a direct § 1090 'financially interested' predicate in its own right, grounded in community-property law (Fam. Code § 760) and, if accurately recalled, precedent applying that theory to void a public contract benefiting a spouse's employer (Fraser-Yamor Agency v. County of Del Norte, cite unverified). That reframes the analysis: even if Okafor's own board seat is separately cured via a § 1091.5(a) nonprofit-officer noninterest, that cure does not reach the distinct, likely-uncured spousal-salary interest — and the proposition's facts keep both the board seat and the spousal job intact, changing nothing but recusal. The proposition's disjunctive 'void or voidable' framing is also a comparatively easy bar to clear. I stay below the top of the panel's range because the exact statutory text of § 1091(b)'s remote-interest categories and § 1091.5(a)'s conditions remains unverified by me, and a non-frivolous 'fixed, non-contingent salary is too attenuated to be an interest at all' counterargument survives every panelist's own scrutiny, including mine.
rationales: Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 and Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565 establish that § 1090's prohibition attaches to the body's act of contracting whenever any member has a disqualifying interest, so an interested member's recusal from the vote does not itself cure the defect absent a clean statutory remote-interest or noninterest exception — this structural point, present in my round-1 answer, was reinforced rather than undercut by the panel's unanimous agreement (7/7).

Community-property law (Fam. Code § 760, and per some panelists Fam. Code § 751) plausibly supplies a direct theory that Okafor herself is 'financially interested' in the contract because her spouse's salary is community property in which Okafor has a present, equal interest — this is a stronger and more direct route to a § 1090 interest than treating spousal income as relevant only to the Political Reform Act's § 87103 materiality analysis, and if Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of Del Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201 in fact applied this theory to void a contract benefiting a spouse's employer (citation and holding unverified), that is squarely on point.

Separating the two interests analytically (as several panelists did) clarifies the outcome: even assuming Okafor's own uncompensated board seat independently qualifies for a § 1091.5(a) nonprofit-officer/director noninterest, that exception is personal to her governance role and does not extend to cure the distinct financial interest created by her spouse's compensated employment with the same contracting nonprofit — and the proposition's facts do not include any change to that employment, only recusal.

The spouse's role as Director of Programs (a management/policy-adjacent position) makes the 'fixed salary wholly independent of the contract' characterization needed for a § 1091(b) remote-interest cure harder to sustain than it would be for a rank-and-file employee, especially layered onto the 40%-of-budget/60%-win-rate revenue-dependence facts, which push the interest toward 'direct and material' rather than remote or speculative under the strict-construction rule of Stigall and People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289.

The proposition is phrased disjunctively ('void or voidable'), which is an easier bar to satisfy than a pure voidness claim; combined with § 1092's remedial structure and § 1097's criminal exposure for willful violations (both of which exist precisely because the Legislature intended prophylactic, not merely compensatory, consequences), this modestly raises the probability that some legally cognizable defect attaches to the contract as structured, even if courts might differ on the void-versus-voidable characterization.
data.biggest_uncertainty: I cannot independently verify (a) the exact text and conditions of Gov. Code § 1091(b)'s enumerated remote-interest categories to confirm whether any cleanly covers 'employee of a contracting nonprofit, compensation independent of the contract,' (b) the precise conditions of § 1091.5(a)'s nonprofit-officer/director noninterest exception and whether it forecloses reliance on it when a spouse is separately and materially compensated by the same entity, and (c) the actual holding and correct citation of Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of Del Norte, which the panel repeatedly invoked (with some internal disagreement over the case name) as direct authority applying community-property theory to void a § 1090 contract via a spouse's employer — if that case does not actually stand for that proposition, a meaningful part of my upward revision loses its footing.

Fam. Code §§ 760 and 751 (community property in spousal earnings) — relevance to § 1090 'financially interested' standard, not just PRA, needs doctrinal confirmation

data.response_to_panel: Two considerations genuinely moved me. First, the panel's framing that community-property law (Fam. Code § 760) supplies a direct § 1090 'financially interested' theory — not merely a Political Reform Act materiality argument — was a real gap in my round-1 reasoning, where I had parked Fam. Code § 760 as 'relevant to the PRA analysis, not the § 1090 holding.' If courts treat spousal income as the official's own interest for § 1090 purposes (as Fraser-Yamor arguably held, though unverified), that meaningfully strengthens the case that an interest exists at all, which was my own stated principal source of doubt. Second, the panel's cleaner analytical separation — board seat possibly cured under § 1091.5(a), but that cure not reaching the distinct spousal-salary interest — is a sharper and more defensible structure than my round-1 treatment, which conflated uncertainty about the board seat with uncertainty about the spousal interest. I did not move all the way to the panel mean/median because the core statutory-text uncertainty (exact § 1091(b)/§ 1091.5(a) conditions) that anchored my round-1 score is unresolved by the summary itself — several panelists flagged the same gap — and the 7/7 acknowledged counterargument (fixed salary as too attenuated to be a disqualifying interest) still represents a live, non-frivolous path to the proposition being false that I'm not willing to discount below roughly one-in-four.
